title: Westwood
text:
FILM REVIEW
    WESTWOOD
    Punk. Icon. Activist.
    
    
      DIR. LORNA TUCKER
      ·
      DOCUMENTARY
      ·
      REVIEW BY KATHLEEN JUAREZ
    
  

  
  
    
      “When does someone cease to be a daring provocateur,and become museum worthy?”
    
  




















  
  

    For those unfamiliar with Vivienne Westwood save for her moniker on a clothing label, the designer might strike you as a demure British dame within the first few minutes of director Lorna Tucker's Westwood. Lounging in a tufted chair next to an exquisite floral arrangement, Westwood seems sweet in grandmotherly way, if not eccentric.

    Fast-forward to a few minutes later into the documentary and Westwood leaves you with a very different impression of the designer. The night before her London Fashion Week show, she goes over the minutia of each piece, model, and accessory. Nothing escapes her notice and she berates an assistant for making an egregious error: a judgment call for a small hem on a garment where a large ribbed hem should be. "I don't remember doing this. Fuck them, they're just disgusting. What's with this tiny hem? It's crap," she says.

    
      
      "I don't know what I'm doing, it's a mess. It's my fault, it's just not good."
      — Vivienne Westwood
    

    Director Lorna Tucker's talent comes in the keen juxtaposition of the two sides of Westwood's demeanor. While the documentary is laden with incidences of Westwood's ferocity, it's contrasted with more intimate moments of Westwood at work. Camera panned at a distance, she confides in her husband post tiny-hem tirade, "I don't know what I'm doing, it's a mess. It's my fault, it's just not good." We can see where the weight of her work weighs heavily on her shoulders.

    Several times throughout the documentary, Westwood iterates a version of "If I don't like it, I just won't put it out." Though her desire for complete creative control leads her to be tyrannical at times, Westwood is revealed to be a deeply sensitive, and introspective artist who pours herself into every detail of her work.

    
      ✦
    

    Westwood's work is one of constant rebellion both against the status quo of stuffy English society and at certain points even her own previous designs. One portion of the documentary shows a museum curator from The Victoria and Albert Museum delicately handling Westwood's most famous piece: a muslin t-shirt adorned with a swastika and other subversive imagery. In another moment of brilliant juxtaposition, tinkling classical piano music plays as the curator runs her gloved hand over the inverted cross printed on the shirt.

    The emotional weight of the documentary relies on Westwood examining this question herself. Much of the documentary seems to be Westwood grappling with her name, company, and designs becoming globally known and marketable while still maintaining her artistic integrity as her company is on the verge of becoming a leviathan. "I'm in danger of not being able to control everything properly," she laments.

    
      VERDICT
      Perhaps most poignant about this documentary is the fact that despite having essentially built her career rebelling against the status quo, later Westwood is ironically resistant to change, evident in her fight against climate change and the uncontrollable growth of her own company.
      Overall the film succeeds in taking Vivienne Westwood into the third dimension. The designer is a deeply complicated character and Lorna Tucker takes great care in exploring the intricacies of the designer's psyche over the course of the documentary. Through Tucker's lens, Westwood is more than just a label on a tag; she's a true artist willing to sacrifice monetary gain and fame to maintain her own creative vision.
    

  

  
    
    Written by Kathleen Juarez

title: resume-Kathleen
text:
Professional Summary

A digital marketer with a writer’s instinct and an analyst’s curiosity, using data to tell better stories and make smarter marketing decisions. 

My interests lie in digital marketing strategy, audience engagement, and campaign performance, understanding how people discover, interact with, and respond to digital content. What sets my approach apart is an analytical perspective: I enjoy using tools such as Google Analytics 4, Hubspot CRM, and R to explore customer behavior and uncover insights that help refine messaging, improve digital experiences, and guide smarter marketing decisions. 


My professional background spans communications, marketing, and event coordination, where I’ve managed multi-channel campaigns and projects from concept through execution — developing content, coordinating across teams, and tracking performance to measure what works. These experiences shaped my interest in the convergence of creative messaging, audience connection, and measurable outcomes. I’m drawn to the intersection where a well-told story meets a well-read dataset. That’s where the most interesting marketing problems live and where I do my best work.




Skills

Digital Marketing & Campaigns

Multi-channel campaign planning, execution & performance optimization
Social media content, management & digital storytelling
Email marketing, automation & brand messaging
Database marketing & audience segmentation

Content Creation & Design

Adobe Creative Suite (Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign)
Canva, CapCut (video editing)
HTML/CSS fundamentals (website & email editing)
Writing — email, social media, web, newsletters, presentations, editorial, promotional, film & TV scripts

Marketing Technology

HubSpot CRM (segmentation, workflows & automation)
Google Analytics 4 (GA4)
SEO/SEM — SEMrush, Moz
Website CMS & content management

Analytics & Reporting

Audience engagement metrics & conversion analysis
Marketing forecasting & performance analysis
R, SQL/BigQuery & data visualization

Applied AI for Marketing

AI-assisted campaign optimization & content development
Predictive modeling for marketing performance
AI-powered digital marketing workflows

[2019 – Present]

Designed and executed multi-channel campaigns across email, print, and digital platforms to drive community engagement and connection.
Built and managed audience segmentation strategies in HubSpot CRM, using engagement metrics to refine messaging, optimize timing, and improve communication effectiveness.
Produced branded content including newsletters, social media graphics, flyers, and event materials to support engagement initiatives and maintain consistent storytelling.
Collaborated cross-functionally with internal teams and stakeholders to align messaging, coordinate timelines, and support property-wide communications.




Editorial Intern, Flaunt Magazine
[2018]

Wrote and edited editorial content aligned with brand voice, increasing audience engagement and relevance.
Supported content production, event coordination, and stakeholder communication for brand activations and media initiatives.
Ensured brand consistency through fact-checking, proofreading, and adherence to editorial standards



Summer Day Camp Director and Grant Writer, YMCA of West San Gabriel Valley
[2014 – 2017]

Directed internal communications and coordinated daily schedules for 200+ campers and a team of 20+ staff, ensuring consistent messaging and smooth operations.
Researched, budgeted, and wrote grants, and supported implementation once funds were received, contributing to program funding and sustainability.
Composed organizational documents and correspondence, supporting clear communication across staff, stakeholders, and leadership.
Led recruitment marketing efforts for seasonal staff, including crafting job postings and screening applicants.
Planned, scheduled, and executed field trips and daily events, balancing logistics, vendor coordination, and budget adherence.

title: Tenant Email Notification System
text:
Project Overview

Property Management Office previously relied on a fully manual email workflow: contacts were individually entered into Outlook folders based on tenant self-submitted forms, with no automated way to update, remove, or segment contacts. This project replaced that system end-to-end with a HubSpot CRM, delivering a dynamic, compliant, and analytics-ready email notification platform.

title: Tenant Email Notification System
text:
Tools & Technologies







Tool
Role




HubSpot
CRM, contact management, email builder, analytics


Bitly
URL shortening for printed materials and QR codes


Microsoft Excel
Data export / migration from Outlook


Outlook
Legacy system (replaced)





  
    ⚠️
    The Problem
    
      Static contact lists required constant manual upkeep
      No automated bounce-back removal
      Opt-outs required emailing staff directly
      General and sensitive notifications shared the same list
      No open/click tracking or delivery analytics
    
  
  
    ✅
    The Solution
    
      HubSpot CRM with dynamic, self-updating contact lists
      Custom email sign-up form with automatic contact creation
      Resubmission auto-updates existing contacts
      Personalization tokens (e.g., tenant name in greeting)
      Built-in unsubscribe, mobile-optimized templates

title: Tenant Email Notification System
section: The Challenge: A Broken Manual System
text:
The Challenge: A Broken Manual System
The legacy process for managing tenant communications was outdated and created significant operational overhead and compliance risk.

title: Tenant Email Notification System
text:
The Solution: HubSpot CRM Pipeline

  
    1
    
      Data Migration
      Existing Outlook contacts exported to .xls and bulk-imported into HubSpot, preserving all prior tenant records.
    
  
  →
  
    2
    
      Sign-up Form
      A HubSpot-hosted form published via QR code and short link (Bitly) captures new subscribers automatically.
    
  
  →
  
    3
    
      Dynamic Lists
      Contacts are searchable and filterable. Form submissions auto-create or auto-update records — no manual entry.
    
  
  →
  
    4
    
      Email Campaigns
      One-time blasts or automated sequences with personalization tokens, branded templates, and compliance-ready unsubscribe links.
    
  
  →
  
    5
    
      Analytics
      Delivery, open rate, and click-through rate tracked per campaign — closing the loop on what actually reaches tenants.
    
  


Key Feature Highlights
Dynamic Contact Management HubSpot’s list engine means contacts are always current. Resubmitting the sign-up form automatically updates an existing record rather than creating a duplicate — solving the stale-data problem that plagued Outlook folders.
Compliant Opt-Out Every email includes a one-click unsubscribe footer, meeting CAN-SPAM requirements without requiring tenants to contact the office.
Personalization Tokens Email greetings auto-populate with the tenant’s name, making mass communications feel more personal and increasing engagement.
Delivery Analytics The HubSpot dashboard tracks delivery status, open rates, and click-through rates per campaign, giving the property management team concrete feedback on communication effectiveness.
Campus-Wide Rollout The launch used a printed flyer with QR code, a Bitly short link for digital sharing, and an introductory email to the existing list — all inviting tenants to opt in to the new system.

title: Tenant Email Notification System
text:
Outcomes & Impact

  
    ⏱️
    Time Saved
    Eliminated manual contact entry and bounce-back monitoring — routine list maintenance now requires no staff intervention.
  
  
    📈
    Better Reach
    The QR-code and short-link sign-up flow makes it easy to grow the list beyond the original tenant base to campus employees and visitors.
  
  
    🔍
    Visibility
    For the first time, the team can see open rates, click rates, and delivery stats — enabling data-driven improvements to future campaigns.
  
  
    ⚖️
    Compliance
    Built-in unsubscribe links and opt-in forms align the system with email marketing best practices and CAN-SPAM requirements.

title: About This Site
text:
Jack of all trades with a Masters of Science
Peep that code:


This portfolio was designed and hand-coded by me using Quarto, HTML, and CSS — no templates or drag-and-drop builders involved.






NoteWhy hand-code it?



I wanted full control over the design and to demonstrate that my marketing skills are backed by real technical ability.





Design — layout, color, and typography decisions made from scratch
Structure — built with Quarto’s publishing framework
Styling — custom CSS for things like the pink tooltip you just hovered over 😉







Tool
Purpose




Quarto
Site framework


CSS
Custom styling


GitHub
Version control & hosting
